text: We present a case report of a sales approach for a spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy. This was performed at the Instituto Nacional de Ciencias Médicas y Nutrición Salvador Subirán by the Endocrine and Advanced Laparoscopic Surgery Service. The case is a forty-six year old female who was previously healthy and has had no surgeries. Her main complaint was hypoglycemia, which was manifested by weakness, fatigue, and seizures. Her blood glucose levels have been as low as forty-four and her plasmatic insulin level was a hundred and eighty during fasting.

An abdominal CT reveals a 20mm lesion in the pancreatic tail. The patient is positioned supine on a split leg table, and the surgeon stands between the legs. A 25mm incision is made, separating subcutaneous tissues down to the fascia. The SILS platform is placed, and three trocars are advanced into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic angle to expose the posterior aspect of the pancreas. Using graspers and a harmonic scalpel, the splenic vein and artery are identified and dissected, creating a window for pancreatic division with an endoscopic linear stapler. The pancreas is then completely separated from the splenic vessels.

The surgeon retrieves the pancreatic specimen using an endoscopic bag. Fibrin blue is applied to seal the pancreatic stump, followed by the application of an absorbable hemostat. A twelve French silicone drain is placed and extracted through the single incision.

This chart shows insulin levels during dissection and after extraction of the tumor. Pathology reported the tumor as an insulinoma. The patient was discharged on day six after surgery. On day twelve post-op, the patient presented with fever. A CT scan showed a small collection that was successfully drained.
